title: Non-root containers and pod securityContext
summary: Running as non-root reduces blast radius if a workload is compromised and aligns with pod security standards.
body:
Processes that run as UID 0 inside the container can modify filesystems, install tooling, or exploit kernel interfaces available to privileged users. Production services should declare a non-zero runAsUser or rely on a restricted profile that sets runAsNonRoot: true at the pod level.

Pair with readOnlyRootFilesystem where feasible and drop Linux capabilities so even a compromised app has a minimal attack surface. AegisML flags manifests that lack explicit non-root enforcement on the baseline Deployment.

steps: Set pod.spec.securityContext.runAsNonRoot: true or runAsUser to a non-zero UID supported by your image.
Ensure the container image is built for that UID (USER directive or numeric user in OCI config).
Validate with kubectl exec and id; document exceptions in your risk register.
remediation.example_manifest_snippet:
securityContext:
  runAsNonRoot: true
  runAsUser: 65532
containers:
  - name: app
    securityContext:
      allowPrivilegeEscalation: false
      readOnlyRootFilesystem: true
remediation.verification: kubectl get pod -o jsonpath='{.items[*].spec.securityContext}' and container securityContext; confirm no privileged: true without approval.

nist.name: Least Privilege
nist.discussion: Workload identity should use the minimum privileges required.

nist.name: Least Functionality
nist.discussion: Disable unnecessary capabilities and privileged modes.

fedramp.control: SC-4 (Information in Shared Resources) — process isolation
fedramp.notes: Non-root execution supports shared-cluster tenancy narratives in the SSP.
